what_it_does: Acceptance tests for the bounded routine goal-loop runner (runtime.routine_runner.run_goal_loop). It monkeypatches RR.fire with mock implementations to verify stop-on-met, context passing between rounds, a hard max_rounds ceiling, regex-style done_when matching, and fail-loud behavior for missing stop conditions or invalid max_rounds.
observations: Monkeypatches runtime.routine_runner.fire and restores it at the end
Uses a PASS/FAIL list with a custom check() helper instead of pytest assertions
Defines mock fire functions that return 'AGAIN' then 'DONE', always 'nope', or a regex-matching string
Tests context-passing by inspecting the prompt_override string passed to the second mock fire call
Includes explicit fail-loud tests for missing done_when/evaluator and for max_rounds < 1
Stated scope is S-R9.1 / CC-22 goal-loop acceptance without real spawns
standouts: Hard max_rounds ceiling is tested by counting exact mock fire invocations
Regex done_when syntax '/.../' is exercised in an acceptance test
Fail-loud design is treated as a safety requirement: 'never unbounded'
conventions: sys.path manipulation to import runtime modules
Original function reference saved and restored after monkeypatching
concerns: Tests share global monkeypatch state; restore() only runs at the very end, so an early exception could leave RR.fire mocked
Test 3 is a no-op assertion (True) rather than verifying the source argument
Test 2's context-passing check depends on the exact string formatting of the next prompt
The regex test uses a lambda fire with no call-count verification
notes: A hand-rolled acceptance test that mocks the runner's fire boundary to prove the goal loop is bounded, context-aware, and fail-loud.
questions: What exact prompt format does run_goal_loop use when appending prior results?
Does RoutineFireError message text remain stable for these error cases?
Is run_goal_loop expected to accept both literal and regex done_when strings?
purpose_vs_actual: Matches: the file tests exactly the bounded loop behaviors described in its docstring.
intention_signals: To provide confidence that the goal-loop runner cannot run unbounded, always knows when to stop, passes prior results forward, and surfaces configuration errors loudly rather than silently looping.
novelty: Treats 'never unbounded' as a first-class acceptance requirement and tests it through exact invocation counting; also tests regex goal-matching syntax in an integration-style acceptance file.
contribution: An executable contract for run_goal_loop behavior: stop-on-met, hard max_rounds enforcement, inter-round context passing, regex done_when evaluation, and fail-loud handling of invalid or incomplete loop configuration.
summary_for_embedding: Acceptance test file that mocks the routine runner's fire function to verify bounded goal-loop behavior including stop-on-met, context passing, max_rounds enforcement, regex done_when matching, and fail-loud invalid configurations.
intention_for_embedding: Provide S-R9.1 acceptance coverage for the goal-loop runner without using real spawns.

symbols.does: Test assertion helper that records pass/fail status and prints the result.
symbols.description: check(n, c, d='') appends the test name n to PASS or FAIL based on condition c, prints a PASS/FAIL line, and optionally prints detail d when the check fails.

symbols.does: Restores the original runtime.routine_runner.fire function after monkeypatching.
symbols.description: restore() reassigns RR.fire to the previously saved _orig reference, used as teardown for the acceptance tests.

symbols.does: Mock fire implementation that returns 'AGAIN' on the first call and 'DONE' on the second.
symbols.description: mock_two(routine, base, turn_timeout, prompt_override, source) records each prompt_override in calls and returns a result that lets the goal loop stop on round 2, enabling tests of stop-on-met and context-passing.

symbols.does: Mock fire implementation that always returns a non-matching result.
symbols.description: never(routine, base, turn_timeout, prompt_override, source) appends a marker and returns {'result': 'nope', 'is_error': False}, forcing the loop to hit its hard max_rounds ceiling.

symbols.does: Helper that checks whether a callable raises RoutineFireError containing a substring.
symbols.description: raises(fn, sub) runs fn() and returns True only if it raises RR.RoutineFireError and the exception message contains sub; used to assert fail-loud behavior.
